data.title: Starwood Property Trust: Buy This 9% Yield For Recurring Income
data.description:
Most investors who follow the market know that it returns on average 9-10% annually. However, it would be naïve to expect that this will be exactly what it returns every year, on a consistent basis. That’s because the 9-10% is just an average with plenty of volatility baked in. This means that in some years the return could be significantly higher and in others, substantially lower than the average.

While being invested in an index fund like the S&P 500 (SPY) may make sense for some investors who can stomach volatility, those who prize more steady cash flow may opt for alternatives. This brings me to Starwood Property Trust (NYSE:STWD), which I last covered in July, highlighting the attractiveness of its diversified portfolio, growing data center exposure, and appealing valuation. STWD has since remained range-bound, trading mostly in the $20-21 range. At the current price of $20.30, STWD looks appealing with a 9.5% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit STWD, including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently an attractive ‘Buy’ for its high-yielding income potential, so let’s get started!

Why STWD?

Starwood Property Trust is one of the largest commercial mortgage REITs in the U.S. It operates a diverse portfolio across commercial real estate loans, loan servicing, infrastructure and residential loans, and physical property ownership. This makes it more diversified than peer Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), which focuses on commercial mortgages. Since inception in 2009, STWD has deployed $108 billion in total capital across its business segments.

At present, STWD has a $27.5 billion portfolio value, with just over half (56%) invested in commercial mortgages. Just 9% of the total portfolio is related to U.S. office. As shown below, about one-third of the portfolio is invested in infrastructure and residential lending, and physical properties, thereby mitigating risk from any one business segment.

STWD demonstrated solid operating results during Q2 2025. This included distributable EPS of $0.49 stemming from the commercial & residential lending segment alone. Additionally, the REIS (investing and servicing) segment delivered $0.15 distributable EPS, and the property and infrastructure lending segments delivered another $0.21 distributable EPS.

It’s worth noting that total distributable EPS came in at $0.43, which sits under the $0.48 quarterly dividend rate. This was due to a $44 million distributable earnings (DE) loss as it relates to the resolution of its apartment building in Northlake, Texas. It’s worth noting that while DE took a hit, STWD realized a $4 million GAAP gain after selling it at its cost basis, so there was no loss. I would expect for distributable EPS to bounce back (and for dividend coverage to improve) as STWD redeploys proceeds from this sale.

Speaking of which, STWD isn’t seeing a shortage of investment opportunities, as it committed $3.2 billion toward new investments during the second quarter. This includes $1.9 billion dedicated to commercial lending, of which $500 million was tied to two fully pre-leased data center projects. Another $700 million was dedicated to infrastructure lending. This represents a material acceleration in investments compared to $2.3 billion commitments during Q1.

At the same time, STWD made progress in resolving challenged office and multifamily loans, with two foreclosed assets sold for $115 million. Also encouraging, STWD’s property portfolio benefited from HUD-authorized rent increases averaging 8%. Plus, the infrastructure lending segment is one of the fastest-growing segments, reaching $3.1 billion after $642 million of funded originations during the last quarter.

Looking ahead, STWD is well-positioned to create long-term value. This includes its net lease properties acquisition, which is expected to be modestly dilutive this year, but increasingly accretive thereafter. This echoes the trajectory of STWD’s infrastructure lending business. That business was acquired in 2018 and has since been scaled into a compelling platform with robust growth and long-term drivers.

I would expect for data center growth to be a tailwind for STWD as it’s currently finding a healthy flow of opportunities. As shown below, the U.S. data center market is expected to grow at a 10.7% CAGR between now and the end of the decade.

Moreover, management expects U.S. CRE transaction volumes to improve as lower rates should aid refinancing activity and improve outcomes for challenged loans in the portfolio. Management expects SOFR rates to decline by 3% by early 2027, and as shown below, the total U.S. real estate market is expected to grow at a 4.1% CAGR through 2030.

STWD is supported by a sound balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 2.5x. This sits just above the historically low leverage ratio of 2.25x from the first quarter. STWD also has robust liquidity of $1.1 billion and no debt maturities for the remainder of the year and just $400 million of maturing debt next year.

Lastly, I see value in STWD at the current price of $20.30, which equates to just a 3% premium to its undepreciated book value. This slight premium could be made up by just over one quarter’s worth of dividends. Plus, I see potential for book value accretion with continued growth in the physical property ownership and infrastructure lending segment.

Risks to STWD include its exposure to office and multifamily, which have been hit with increased remote work and higher supply, respectively. This could result in unforeseen credit defaults. Moreover, STWD faces counterparty risk as it relies on banks and capital markets for financing. As such, available capital could dry up during a credit crunch, making it harder for STWD to refinance debt.

Investor Takeaway

Starwood Property Trust offers an appealing opportunity for income investors seeking high-yield alternatives to traditional index funds. It has a well-diversified $27.5 billion portfolio spanning commercial and residential lending, infrastructure, and physical assets, enabling it to spread its risk.

STWD continues to redeploy capital effectively, particularly into fast-growing sectors like data centers and infrastructure lending. It’s supported by strong liquidity and modest leverage. STWD looks attractive for investors with a 9.5% dividend yield and trading at just a slight premium to book value. As such, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on the stock for its high income.

data.title: The Current Round Of Rate Cuts Will Likely Spur The Global De-Dollarization Trend
data.description:
Raising prices on shopworn merchandise because the family business isn’t making ends meet is not a recipe for success. But the Fed and the Treasury Department are creating a similar scenario as they gamble that institutional investors at home and abroad will love their repeatedly downgraded Treasury bonds all the more as they raise the price.

On Wednesday (September 17), the Fed agreed to drop rates by 25 bps and indicated two more cuts were in the works before the end of the year. US Treasuries’ biggest foreign investors are losing interest as it is. As you can see from the chart below, China’s holdings of US Treasuries are down 43% from their peak in 2013. Japan, one of the US’s staunchest allies, has also reduced its holdings, but not for political reasons. Japan has its own financial woes to consider, and loading up on increasingly downgraded US bonds doesn’t seem like a good idea right now.

Numerous other countries, mostly European, have increased their Treasury holdings, which has mitigated some of the effect. Nevertheless, foreign holdings of US debt have declined from 35% in 2015 to roughly 23% by early 2025.

The Global De-Dollarization Trend

In response to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the US government’s sweeping sanctions against Russia and its trading partners have triggered a blowback in the form of the global de-dollarization trend. A subtle yet steady shift away from the US dollar in global trade is now in motion. How far this will go and where it all ends is anyone’s guess.

America’s Foreign Investors Are Exchanging Dollars For Gold

As governments around the world shed bonds, they are loading up on gold at an accelerating pace. Central banks are adding more than 1,000 tons of gold to their reserves annually. A recent survey conducted by the World Gold Council revealed that 95% of central bank respondents believe global gold reserves will rise over the coming year. This appears to be the driving force behind the sudden rise in the value of gold.

China’s Belt and Road Initiative

US sanctions against Russia, India, China, and numerous other countries could not have come at a better time for China’s Belt and Road Initiative. The China International Payments System [CIPS], launched in 2015, has steadily gained momentum in the years since the sanctions went into effect. Since 2021, CIPS transactions have gone from 80 trillion yuan to 175.5 trillion yuan—a 119% increase.

Fig. 2

Meanwhile, China has signed more than $500 billion worth of currency swap agreements with 40 countries and counting. The Chinese payment system now connects more than 1,300 financial institutions, processing over $12 trillion annually across 100 countries.

Russia’s International Payment System

Russia is also getting into the act. In response to being shut out of the Euro-based SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) international payment system, Russia is fostering a similar system known as SPFS (System for Transfer of Financial Messages). SPFS, created to bypass Western financial systems and sanctions, now connects over 400 users. Although its global reach is not yet as large as the SWIFT system, China, Turkey, Kazakhstan, India, and Iran have all signed on to participate.

The Power of US Financial Sanctions May Have Reached Its Limits

The US is currently sanctioning more countries now than at any time in our history. The original idea behind US sanctions was to isolate countries working at cross-purposes to the United States and thereby bring them in line. But since the Ukraine war, so many sanctions have been meted out to so many nations, there is a possibility that the US is simply isolating itself and thereby diminishing its global power.

A flurry of punitive US tariff transactions, matched with a policy of an ICE arrest quota of 3,000 immigrants a day, clearly isn’t helping. The global de-dollarization trend is likely to continue throughout the current administration. Whether the US dollar can regain its lost prominence in the world marketplace in the near future is an open question.

Why The Fed Is Cutting Now

It seems odd for the Trump administration to insist on a large decline in rates even as the stock market soars to new highs. But what’s not being said out loud is that these new highs are not the result of a strong economy. The soaring stock market has far outstripped any increase in economic output.

Fig. 3

Since emerging from the last recession in 2009, our economy has grown by 102%. In that same amount of time, the S&P 500 has risen 890%, an obvious mismatch and one more indicator that the stock market is currently overpriced.

The official reason given for this upcoming rate cut is a minor dip in the number of jobs added to the economy in August—only 22,000, less than the 79,000 jobs gained in the previous month. Over the same month, the rate of unemployment rose from 4.2% to 4.3%, a 0.1% increase. This is hardly a reasonable excuse to ignore the fact that the stock market is way overpriced, let alone the risk of stagflation.

The Fed’s Rinse and Repeat Rate Cuts Could Render The Same Dubious Results as Last Time

The last time the Fed lowered the Federal Fund Rate, the 10-year Treasury—that benchmark rate that sets the rates for mortgages, auto loans, credit cards, and numerous other financial transactions—did something completely unexpected. Rather than dipping from the gravitational force of a Federal Funds rate lowered by 100 bps over the course of three rate cuts, the 10-year Treasury soared over 120 bps. It didn’t settle down until President Trump began insisting on further rate cuts in January 2025. It seems bond traders are employing a strategy commonly known as ‘buy the rumor and sell the news.’ Notice in the chart below that even though rates have been declining again in anticipation of more rate cuts, the 10-year Treasury never did drop down to where it was before the last set of rate cuts a year ago.

Fig. 4

At the beginning of 2025, there was much head-scratching on Wall Street as everyone from home buyers to credit card shoppers complained of ever-increasing rates, an affront considering they had been promised the opposite.

More recently, long-term bonds experienced a sugar rush as rates dipped down in the lead-up to Wednesday’s rate cut. But today the bond market is experiencing a morning-after effect as the crucial benchmark 10-year Treasury started off the trading day with a 1% increase. Considering this is exactly what it did in response to the last round of rate cuts, this should not come as a surprise. But even now, the markets are anticipating lower mortgage and auto loan rates that will likely never materialize.

Fig. 5

Risks Involved in Selling Rather Than Buying Long-Term Bonds

In most cases, I am generally very honest about the risks involved in whatever investment suggestions I make. But this time it is difficult to form an argument, aside from the age-old ‘This time it’s different.’ The last set of rate cuts in 2024 fully established that the Fed no longer commands the power to influence rates in long-term bonds by cutting the Federal Funds rate. The only way the Fed could possibly get those long-term rates down at this point in time is to conjure trillions of dollars out of thin air and throw it at the bond market, that is, purchase trillions of dollars of US bonds. If the Fed takes this course—and if President Trump gets his people on the Fed board, it is a very likely scenario—the long-term bond rates would indeed be forced down, if only for a year or so. But, in terms of sound fiscal policy, that’s the equivalent of a kitchen fire sending flames shooting through the roof. Hyper-stagflation would be the likely result.

Summary:

Fiscal policymakers at the Fed and the Treasury Department appear dangerously oblivious to the diminishing power that the US government commands in the global economy. Another round of ineffective rate cuts is likely to weaken that influence all the more, as the bond market makes it obvious that the US cannot get its fiscal house in order. The proverbial elephant in the room is the fact that institutional bond traders, both domestic and abroad, are generally the most knowledgeable and sophisticated of all the investors. They know more than anyone that raising the price of bonds that have been repeatedly downgraded for very good reasons makes those bonds weaker, not stronger. For now, we should keep our eyes on the 10-year Treasury. It will be interesting to see how this all plays out.

data.title: Fed Cuts Its Target Interest Rate And Signals More To Come
data.description:
By James Picerno

US Treasury yields rose across the yield curve on Thursday, a day after the Federal Reserve cut its target rate for the first time this year. The biggest increases were at the longer end of the curve, which is more sensitive to inflation risk.

The increase in market rates suggests lingering investor concern that inflation will remain well above the Fed’s 2% target, in part because softer monetary policy could make it harder to close the gap in the near term.

In yesterday’s press conference, Fed Chairman Powell recognized that “We have begun to see goods prices showing through into higher inflation, and actually the increase in goods prices accounts for most of the increase in inflation, or perhaps all of the increase in inflation over the course of this year.” He added that the effects for the economy “are not very large at this point,” although “we do expect them to continue to build over the course of the rest of the year and into next year.”

Taking a cautious view on the prospects that inflation will remain contained, much less fall, the US 10-year Treasury yield rose on Thursday to 4.11%, the highest since Sep. 4. The policy-sensitive 2-year yield also increased. Morningstar notes that it’s “highly unusual” for Treasury rates to rise so quickly after the Fed begins a rate-cutting cycle.

The path of least resistance is still down for the Fed target rate

The benchmark rate remains well below the previous peak in February (roughly 4.80%), and so yesterday’s pop in market yields could turn out to be noise. The case for expecting interest rates to slide further draws on several sources, starting with the Fed’s new projections. Members of the central bank’s policy-setting committee predict the median Fed funds rate will fall to 3.6% this year, slipping to 3.4% in 2026 from the current 4.13% median. Meantime, the Fed funds futures market is pricing in two more ¼-point cuts by the end of the year.

TMC Research’s Fed funds model also indicates space for additional policy easing. After Wednesday’s rate cut, our rough estimate of the neutral rate for the Fed’s target remains well below the 4.13% median - a gap that suggests policy remains relatively tight.

What isn’t factored into the model’s analytics, however, could turn out to be significant, namely: the effects of tariffs on inflation in the months ahead. Modeling this “soft” effect is challenging, since it relies on guesswork rather than hard data.

One of the reasons, arguably the key reason, that the Fed has been slow to cut rates in recent months is the uncertainty surrounding how tariffs will affect pricing pressure and the economy. The extreme, rapid shift in US tariff policy this year has limited, if any, precedent in modern history for analyzing the effects.

One view is that inflation is only starting to edge higher from tariffs. If so, easing monetary policy could exacerbate a new reflationary trend. A conflicting theory is that tariffs will be a headwind for economic growth, if only on the margins - an effect that implies lower rates will be needed as an offsetting factor.

Powell was careful to hedge on the uncertainty issue, explaining on Wednesday that while inflation “remains somewhat elevated relative to our 2% longer-run goal,” the labor market is the higher priority at the moment because “payroll job gains have slowed significantly to a pace of just 29,000 per month over the past three months.” Notably, “labor demand has softened and the recent pace of job creation appears to be running below the 'breakeven' rate needed to hold the unemployment rate constant.”

Still trading in a range

From an investment perspective vis-à-vis the bond market, a crucial question is when/if the 10-year yield breaks out of its recent range. At the moment, the bias still looks heavily skewed to the downside - the benchmark rate is trading near the bottom of its “normal” range, per the top chart below.

To the extent the 10-year rate moves to the upper range of the grey box, inflation concerns would be the likely catalyst. For now, at least, that risk is in remission. A key test of this relatively benign environment for bonds is next month’s September update for consumer inflation (October 15).

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: $100 Silver Could Be Next
data.description:
Silver keeps breaking all records. What could be the next price stop? It is only nearing the levels it was at in 2011 during the Fed's QE program. It was the historical period I have covered in my previous publications. I am sure many investors are wondering what to expect next. After all, it is often a poor idea to buy at multi-year highs. But what if it is just the beginning for the precious metal's rally?

Summary of my previous article about silver

In one of my previous publications about the grey shiny metal titled “Silver Prices Might Still Soar To Triple Digits Despite Trump's Tariffs,” I wrote that Trump's tariffs initially brought silver prices down. However, long-term supply problems and higher demand for safe-haven assets could push prices for the grey shiny metals up.

Among the bullish factors I stated were geopolitical uncertainties and industrial demand from green energy and electronics companies.

Also, despite short-term bearish factors related to market sell-offs, I wrote that in the long term, silver prices would likely increase due to tariff-related supply shortages and rising demand for the metal in the green energy industry.

Since then, the silver ETFs have appreciated by 27.71% as of the time of writing versus the S&P 500’s gain of 23.58%. Most of the rally was due to the Fed’s dovish tone. From a historical perspective, however, the silver prices are still not as high as they used to be in the past.

Reasons for the recent rally

The recent rally has been truly astonishing. Silver is now trading at a price of $42 per ounce, rising from about $30 per ounce, thus surging by 40%.

In other words, it surged as much as gold prices, from $2,600 to about $3,700, by 42%, as of the time of writing.

To start with, gold price moves have obviously been due to the Fed's more dovish attitude towards the interest rates. I explained this in some more detail about gold prices in my recent article, "I Was Right About The Fed And Gold." The same is partly true of silver, even though the shiny grey metal does not enjoy the status of the central banks' preferred asset class, unlike gold. Central banks have been making plenty of yellow metal purchases in the recent couple of years. The diagram below shows central bankers’ recent net purchases of gold. The surge in net buys has been truly astonishing between 2020 and 2025, remarkably rising in 2022.

Gold is considered to be one of the world’s most liquid assets. Only the world’s major currencies, such as the USD and the EUR, are more liquid and therefore held by central bankers as foreign reserves.

Silver, however, is not regarded as an alternative to cash by most governments and central bankers. The notable exception, however, is the PBOC (the central bank of China), which still regards silver as a monetary metal and attempts to keep sufficient reserves of it. Interestingly, China used to be on a silver standard in 1935. In fact, since 1983, together with gold bullion, the Chinese central bank has been controlling the accumulation of the country’s silver reserves.

It is also a metal favored for speculative trading. According to the Silver Institute, the rise in silver demand has been due to silver-backed exchange-traded products (ETPs) and futures trading.

The total net inflows were 95 million ounces in the first 6 months of 2025. The total silver ETP investment has already exceeded the total for the whole of 2024. So, by 30 June this year, global silver ETP holdings totaled 1.13 billion ounces, only 7% below their February 2021 peak.

On the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, as of 24 June, the net long position surged by a whopping 163% compared to the end of 2024 levels. Meanwhile, according to the Silver Institute, in 2025 institutional investors have shown a growing interest in silver as a store of value. All this led to higher prices in 2025 despite a fall in retail investors’ demand.

Upside factors

Here are the reasons for silver’s further upside potential.

First, inflation might keep falling, while unemployment might keep rising, thus pushing the Fed to ease its monetary policies. This is quite a paradoxical situation—silver is an industrial metal. So, a strong economy should be a benefit for its industrial demand. At the same time, there is a possibility for lower activity in the real sector, coupled with the growing popularity of green technologies, a strong upside factor for silver. But as I have explained above, silver is also a monetary metal, which benefits from the dovish Fed. Second, asset bubbles might eventually burst, thus making investors seek more reliable alternatives to park their cash. Moreover, bursting asset bubbles lead to recessions, more dovish central bankers, and therefore higher silver prices. The rise of precious metal standards is also a possibility. In one of my previous publications titled “Gold And Silver Will Soon Be Mediums Of Exchange In Texas,” I wrote that Texas would officially make gold and silver mediums of exchange in 2027, likely inspiring other states and even countries to follow their example, thus boosting demand for precious metals.

Downside risks

Among the downside factors is obviously a less dovish tone from the Fed, which is possible if the US inflation indicators get worse. Also, poor industrial demand for silver is another important risk. The latter is possible if green technologies do not develop as fast as expected, for example. Also, there could be an unexpected rise in supply. But it is not my base-case scenario because silver is mined as a byproduct of other metals, most notably copper, lead, and zinc. So, if the demand for these metals falls, a likely result of an economic slowdown, the supply of silver would fall.

Another bearish factor is investors’ irrational enthusiasm with other asset classes, such as modern technologies. This can lead to silver being underappreciated and ignored by investors versus other investments. I will talk about this in some more detail below.

Current valuations

In my opinion, silver is not as expensive as it looks. Indeed, if we view the silver price graph, the grey metal trades close to its all-time highs.

But it is necessary to adjust historical pricing for inflation in order to view silver in the context of today's money.

As of 1980, when the US struggled with stagflation while the Fed tried to manage the consequences of the gold standard's cancellation, silver prices even exceeded $100 in today's money, which could be the next record high.

But what are high valuations? Isn't the stock market, with US tech on top of it, less overvalued than silver? Not at all. Even though I predicted further gains for the S&P 500 due to the Fed's dovish tone, it does not mean the stock market is not overvalued. Nvidia's (NVDA) capitalization alone makes up more than 13% of the US GDP. Not to mention that the Buffett ratio that measures the stock market cap as a percentage of the US GDP is historically high.

Also, it is very hard to find a US-listed company that would fill Ben Graham's valuation criteria. Analysts associate the current market moves with the situation during the Roaring Twenties or the dot-com bubble. So, compared to the S&P 500's rally, the precious metals seem to be even cheaper. The silver-to-S&P 500 ratio, measuring the relationship between the grey metal and the stock market, seems to suggest just that.

In fact, since 2018 the ratio has been staying close to record lows, suggesting overvalued US equities and low silver prices.

Conclusion

In my view, silver has all the reasons to rally further. It is highly undervalued compared to US shares; there are plenty of macroeconomic factors, including the dovish Fed. Also, green energy demand is here too. But silver has upside potential as a store of value and also as a medium of exchange. In Texas it would have these two functions of money thanks to the local officials’ law. Some states and even countries can follow the example of Texas and also the approach of China’s central bank to silver, thus pushing the prices for the grey metal further up. In fact, if there are monetary changes on the scale they were between 1970 and 1980, we could even see a price of $100 per ounce of silver.

data.title: Kura Oncology Transitioning To Commercial Stage: Why I Choose To Buy
data.description:
Kura Oncology, Inc. (NASDAQ:KURA) stock is having a good month and is now on double-digit gains, although still trading slightly above its 52-week lower range. I believe the market is responding to the fast-approaching U.S. Food and Drug Administration's (FDA) Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) date of November 30, 2025 which is the central catalyst for the company's transformation. This means the market is upbeat on these recent developments. The company’s Q2 update was also positive although the company is still in its pre-commercial stage, yet to generate product sales, but managed to generate revenue from collaborations. I view Kura as a company with strong potential given its huge cash position, differentiated profile, and excellent strategic partnership. I assign a Buy rating on KURA stock despite other potential risks it could face and I'll discuss why.

Kura is in the late clinical-to-commercial transition stage, awaiting FDA approval for its first drug, Ziftomenib. The company's mission is to realize the promise of precision medicines to help patients with cancer lead better, longer lives. The company's novel therapies are designed to target cancer cells selectively, increasing clinical benefit while moving the field forward in areas with the highest unmet needs.

Understanding Kura's Current Model

As I highlighted above, Kura is in its late clinical-to-commercial transition stage. This means it doesn’t sell drugs yet. However, its current income comes from a partnership with Kyowa Kirin, which pays milestone bonuses when key goals are hit. Such milestones include events like trial starts or future FDA approval.

In Q2 2025, Kura reported $15.3 million in revenue and it all came this way, not from product sales. In the near term, Kura has the potential to earn up to $375 million more from these one-off payments.

That said, the real turning point will be late 2025, when the FDA decides on Ziftomenib, its first drug for a type of blood cancer, acute myeloid leukemia (AML). If this is approved, Kura will finally make money from drug sales, with the initial market worth $350-400 million annually in the U.S.

However, longer term, Ziftomenib could expand into newly diagnosed AML, a much larger opportunity of over $7 billion. Kura is also looking forward to developing treatments for stomach cancer, solid tumors and even diabetes. In simple terms, I would describe Kura as a company living on partner funding today but aiming for steady drug sales.

The Kura-Kyowa Kirin Deal

In what I believe was a major win for the company, Kura entered into a global strategic partnership with Kyowa Kirin on November 20, 2024. The purpose of the deal was to co-develop and commercialize ziftomenib, Kura’s menin inhibitor for AML and other blood cancers. Menin is a protein that regulates gene expression, but in cancers it drives abnormal cell growth.

At signing, Kura received a $330 million upfront payment thereby providing significant non-dilutive capital for the company. The deal's potential also includes up to $1.2 billion in milestone payments. Meanwhile, in near-term milestones the deal has the potential to unlock $420 million but that one is tied to key events such as regulatory submissions and product launch.

In the U.S., Kura leads development, manufacturing, and commercialization, with both companies sharing profits and losses 50/50. On the other hand, Kyowa Kirin takes charge outside the U.S., with Kura eligible for tiered double-digit royalties.

The collaboration supports frontline AML trials launching in 2025, expands global reach, secures funding through commercialization, and diversifies Kura’s risk while building a long-term revenue model.

I'm of the belief that without this partnership, Kura would have faced a heavy cash burn, higher dilution from equity raises, and limited global reach, potentially jeopardizing its ability to launch and scale ziftomenib successfully.

To a certain extent, I also think that the 'red flag' in this deal is the potential over-reliance on this single partnership for ex-U.S. revenue. I believe the success of the partnership's execution is a key variable to monitor going forward.

Kura’s Pipeline: AML and Beyond

Kura’s pipeline is anchored by ziftomenib, its selective oral menin inhibitor. The pipeline is further supported by additional programs that expand both within and beyond oncology.

Interestingly for ziftomenib, enrollment in the Phase 2 registration-directed trial for relapsed/refractory (R/R) NPM1-mutant AML has been completed, with a PDUFA date set for November 30, 2025. Kura is also preparing to initiate two Phase 3 COMET-17 frontline trials in 2025, targeting both fit and unfit AML patient populations.

Beyond AML, ziftomenib is being investigated in combination with imatinib for gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) in an ongoing Phase 1 proof-of-concept study. This investigation will inform Kyowa Kirin’s option to co-develop in solid tumors.

In parallel, Kura is also advancing KO-2806, its next-generation farnesyltransferase inhibitor (FTI). This drug is currently in a Phase 1 clinical trial for solid tumors including RAS-mutant cancers, renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC).

Meanwhile, Kura has announced that new KO-2806 data will be presented at ESMO in October 2025. Looking further ahead, the company is also progressing next-generation menin inhibitors for diabetes and cardiometabolic disease, with a candidate already nominated. I believe these programs represent a diversified and strategically staged pipeline for Kura.

How Reliable Can Ziftomenib Be?

I think Ziftomenib, being the first drug expected to generate sales for the company, requires some scrutiny because its failure could shutter all the expected dreams.

The drug is said to be highly competitive compared to current AML treatments.

Ziftomenib targets a specific genetic pathway (menin) implicated in up to 50% of AML cases, unlike chemotherapy, transplants, or FLT3/IDH inhibitors that address narrower patient groups and carry significant toxicity. The drug's strengths lie in precision targeting, a favorable safety profile (fewer cardiac issues and drug interactions) and once-daily oral dosing, making it simpler and safer than IV regimens. IV regimens are treatments delivered directly into veins, usually medications like chemotherapy or fluids.

Most noteworthy, the drug combines effectively with standard therapies, potentially delivering deeper and longer remissions. If approved, I believe that ziftomenib could redefine AML care, positioning itself as a best-in-class therapy with broad adoption potential.

Importantly, Ziftomenib has shown strong achievements beyond regulatory progress, including impressive clinical efficacy and safety. In Phase 1 trials, the drug achieved 100% complete remission in newly diagnosed AML patients with key mutations when combined with chemotherapy, alongside high minimal residual disease (MRD) negativity rates and rapid responses.

Meanwhile, in relapsed/refractory AML, monotherapy produced meaningful remissions, especially at higher doses. Importantly, it demonstrated a favorable safety profile with manageable side effects, minimal QTc (a heart measurement on an ECG) issues, and good tolerability, leading to rapid trial enrollment and strong patient retention.

Overall, I believe Ziftomenib appears effective, safe, and easy to take and works well with other treatments. The drug has the potential to change how AML is treated and Kura will likely experience good market penetration post approval.

Ziftomenib's Key Risks

Despite promising early results, Ziftomenib faces several key risks in my view.

Regulatory approval remains uncertain even with Breakthrough Therapy Designation from the FDA. This means that timelines and final approval outcomes are not guaranteed.

On the other hand, the drug's safety and long-term efficacy are still being evaluated, leaving questions about the durability of response and potential adverse effects.

Meanwhile, market competition is significant, with other menin inhibitors like Syndax Pharmaceuticals' revumenib and Janssen's (Johnson & Johnson) bleximenib progressing through development. Competitors could limit Ziftomenib’s market share if they happen to gain earlier approvals or demonstrate stronger clinical outcomes.

As of November 2024, revumenib has received FDA approval for relapsed/refractory AML with KMT2A translocations, showing about 21% complete or partial remissions and manageable side effects. Meanwhile, Bleximenib remains in Phase 1/2 trials, demonstrating promising antileukemic activity, with low differentiation syndrome and no major cardiac concerns. I believe these products can be notable menin inhibitor competitors to ziftomenib.

Additionally, Kura relies heavily on milestone payments from partnerships to fund operations. This means that delays or hurdles in commercialization could strain finances and impact the company’s ability to fully capitalize on Ziftomenib’s potential.

Kura's Strong Cash Position

Kura's cash position looks strong although its financial performance reflects a notable cash burn. As of June 30, 2025, the company had $630.7 million in cash which is a decrease from $727.4 million as of December 31, 2024. The company's net loss for the second quarter of 2025 was $66.1 million, an increase from a net loss of $50.8 million in the same period last year.

This cash burn is primarily driven by a significant increase in research and development expenses, which rose to $62.8 million from $39.7 million in the previous year. I see such expenses as very normal for a company in Kura's development stage. Despite these expenses, management believes its current cash, along with additional anticipated collaboration funding from Kyowa Kirin, is sufficient to fund its pipeline and commercialization efforts into 2027 and beyond.

Valuation

Kura's valuation, as measured by its Price/Sales (FWD) ratio of 4.87, indicates it is valued higher than the industry average of 3.88 but lower than its competitor, Syndax Pharmaceuticals, at 7.57. I believe this suggests that while the market has a more favorable outlook on Kura's future sales compared to the broader industry, it values Syndax's potential sales even more highly. This valuation places Kura in the middle ground, reflecting both its significant potential and the competitive landscape.

Conclusion

Based on Kura Oncology's solid financial foundation, highlighted by its significant cash position of $630.7 million and a strategic partnership with Kyowa Kirin, I assign a Buy rating to KURA stock. I view the company's pipeline as robust and diversified, anchored by the late-stage asset Ziftomenib, which is poised for a major catalyst with the upcoming FDA decision. While I acknowledge the cash burn and competition, Kura's proactive approach to funding and its differentiated product profile mitigate these risks, positioning it for substantial growth and a successful transition to a commercial-stage company.

data.title: Nebius' Monster Rally Could Be Just The Warm-Up
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Despite the strong bull run, Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) still offers one of the most compelling bullish setups in AI infrastructure. The $17.4 billion Microsoft contract validates it as a credible hyperscale partner, while the $4.2 billion raise at ultra-low coupons provides the liquidity to accelerate growth. ARR is compounding at triple digits, clusters are running at near-peak utilization, and capacity is set to surpass 1 GW by 2026.

Since my lowest point of coverage, the stock is up 354% in under six months, with another 25% in the past month. This flywheel of validation, capital, capacity, and demand positions Nebius as a future hyperscale-grade neocloud leader. Therefore, I reaffirm the strong buy rating, as surging AI demand is expected to more than triple global data center capacity by 2030 and combined with Nebius’ AI-native, bare-metal infrastructure running at near-full utilization, positions the company to capture an outsized share of this unprecedented growth cycle.

Nebius Secures $4.2B War Chest to Power Next Phase of AI Growth

The achievement of raising $4.2 billion of capital with combined equity and low-rate convertible debt is very good news for the company. The timing, just a week after it revealed its hyperscale deal with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) worth $17.4 billion (with an option to extend to $19.4 billion), is a testament to confidence in the marketplace in Nebius’ ability to deliver its strong agenda of growth.

By raising capital now, Nebius decreases execution risk associated with funding its New Jersey campus and its future expansions across Europe and Israel. Observe that debt was priced at attractive 1.0% and 2.75% coupons significantly less than peers like CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) who recently raised funds at near 9%. This reduces Nebius’ cost of interest by hundreds of millions annually and leaves it with more funds to spend buying GPUs and sites and to scale power capacity.

Moreover, the issuance greatly reinforces the balance sheet with available liquidity being propelled to approximately $5 billion. With CapEx guidance being at $2 billion annually, this cushion is critical to sustaining momentum without access to negative dilution over the near term. The transaction is further useful to Nebius’ strategic profile in the neocloud competition by reassuring counterparts like Microsoft that its projects are fully funded.

Overall, this capital infusion provides Nebius with financial flexibility as well as a competitive advantage and provides a foundation for accelerated infrastructure build-out and greater revenue clarity during 2026 and beyond.

Nebius at the Epicenter of a 3.5X AI Data Center Boom

The global data center industry is entering an unprecedented build-out period with capacity demand to grow by over triple from 82 GW in 2025 to 219 GW by 2030. The surprising thing is that AI workloads are propelling virtually all incremental growth with two-thirds being incremental. The industry annually will need to add 13 to 31 GW of new AI capacity from 2025 through 2030, an extraordinary build-out similar to hyperscale campuses breaking out multi-quarterly. This creates a structural supply-demand gap as hyperscalers can't internally satisfy demand and therefore contract out to specialist neoclouds.

Nebius is uniquely positioned to capitalize. Unlike legacy clouds—designed as general-purpose computing clouds, Nebius has designed AI-native infrastructure to the core, bare-metal servers, liquid cooling, and optimized rack density expressly engineered to support NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) newest GPUs. Being at near-full capacity during Q2 2025 means all 1 MW it powers is engaged out of the box, reflecting the acute shortage of AI compute. With Microsoft centering a 19.4 billion-dollar deal at its 300 MW New Jersey plant, Nebius has validation and funds to scale beyond 1 GW of powered connection by 2026.

Microsoft Deal Powers Nebius’ Leap Toward the Gigawatt Era

Nebius’ Microsoft deal is more than a blockbuster revenue win. It readjusts the cost of capital to the company and propels its sprint to gigawatt-scale capacity. By securitizing debt against Microsoft's balance sheet, Nebius raised only $4.2 billion at 1–2.75% coupons. The transaction essentially makes a capital-starved challenger into a financed hyperscaler-like company and de-risks its roadmap to over 1 GW of connected power by the end of 2026.

The demand side is equally vital. Q2 results showed ARR at $439 million, 438% year-over-year ARR growth, with guidance upped to $900 million–$1.1 billion by year-end. Management cited near-peak cluster utilizations with all open capacity being snatched up quickly. As an anchor tenant by virtue of being with Microsoft, Nebius can now meet out capacity to blue-chip corporations and sovereign AI efforts at premium metrics while shortening customer acquisition timelines as Microsoft validation provides due diligence to other prospects.

By year-end 2025, 220 MW of connected power is projected by Nebius and will be anchored by its 300 MW New Jersey campus and supplemented by expansions. Collectively, Microsoft validation builds a recursive loop in which cheaper capital funds capacity expansions, capacity attracts superior demand, demand propels ARR growth, and ARR liberates yet further expansions. That flywheel is propelling Nebius to join the GW club.

The Bear Case: High Hopes, Narrow Margin for Error

Skeptics argue that Nebius’ forward multiples are defying gravity. At a forward Price/Sales ratio near 39x against a sector median of near 3x, the company maintains a high premium. At such valuations, any delayed delivery, escalation of costs, or softening of demand for AI can trigger a steep re-rating. Bears flag the risk of execution slippage, particularly around the flagship Vineland, New Jersey campus, where it must bring on-stream 100 MW of power until the end of 2025 to satisfy Microsoft.

We also have the problem of comparables. CoreWeave had a bigger US presence and had previously demonstrated hyperscale execution in liquid-cooled density nodes tailored for Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 infrastructure. Nebius, in turn, continues to scale its footprint, 220 MW on tap throughout year-end 2025 versus CoreWeave's 1.3 GW after the acquisition of Core Scientific. From this perspective, bulls are paying ahead of delivery, and the euphoria in the market risks baking in flawless execution.

The bear thesis, however, falls short. It considers valuation in static multiples, not the dynamic growth of capacity and ARR. Nebius' revenues of $105.1 million in the second quarter, up more than 600% from last year, suggest demand is consuming capacity as fast as it is being brought online. Even its core AI infrastructure business came in with positive adjusted EBITDA ahead of plan, signaling unit economics are improving, not deteriorating.

Simply put, although Nebius’ multiples appear extremely elevated compared to traditional IT sector norms, they are fair in the scenario Nebius is entering a trillion-dollar cycle for AI infrastructure, where demand remains ahead of supply.

Takeaway

With AI-powered data center demand set to triple plus by 2030, Nebius' AI-native infrastructure and accelerating ARR trajectory make it set to capture an outsized share. Even considering the rally, its flywheel of capital, capacity, and demand has only just begun.

data.title: ProKidney: Slow-Burning 'Buy' Opportunity Based On 2027 Approval Of Kidney Disease Therapy
data.description:
Investment Overview

The last time I covered ProKidney (NASDAQ:PROK), the Winston-Salem, North Carolina based biotech, for Seeking Alpha, it was July, and its share price was skyrocketing, jumping from $0.6, to >$4.5 overnight, after it shared positive data from its Phase 2, REGEN-007 clinical study of its autologous cellular therapy rilparencel, in patients with Chronic Kidney Disease ("CKD") and diabetes.

The news signaled a remarkable turnaround for a company whose shares may have faced delisting from the Nasdaq (listing laws require a price of >$1 to be maintained long-term), only a couple of years after it went public via a merger with a Special Purpose Acquisition Company ("SPAC") in 2022.

ProKidney's losses were related to the fact that the main sponsor of the SPAC - Chamath Palihapitiya - had begun selling stock in the company, and the fact that the company made amendments to its clinical studies, focusing on patients with more advanced forms of kidney disease, partly because management felt that it could have a greater impact as a later-stage therapy, and partly in response to the threat posed by other therapies in earlier-stage setting - for example, in its 2024 annual report/10K filing, management lists the following competitors:

major and specialty pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies, including developers of tubular and glomerular cell drug modulators, e.g., SGLT2 inhibitors, antifibrosis medications, e.g., Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonists (“MRAs”), glucose-dependent insulin release stimulators, e.g., GLP-1 agonists, induced pluripotent cells, other autologous mesenchymal stem cells and mechanical renal assist devices such as implantable and wearable renal dialysis machines, and advances in peritoneal dialysis and home dialysis.

The data shared in July is summarised in the slide below (taken from a recent corporate presentation):

The significance of the data is related to the fact that, as per ProKidney's Q2 2025 quarterly report/10Q submission:

In a recent Type B meeting, the FDA confirmed that the slope of eGFR in patients from the ongoing Phase 3 PROACT 1 study can serve as the surrogate endpoint and primary basis for a Biologics License Application (“BLA”) submission of rilparencel under the accelerated approval pathway. The FDA agreed that a rilparencel effect size (versus sham controls) of at least 1.5 mL/min/1.73m2/year improvement would be an acceptable demonstration of efficacy in the setting of patients receiving appropriate standard of care therapies.

As we can see in the chart above, the results from the Group 1 patient subset easily meet the FDA's criteria for approval, and encouragingly, as ProKidney's CEO Bruce Culleton put it during a recent fireside chat at the Morgan Stanley Healthcare Conference:

The treatment schedule in Group 1 is the same as we have in our Phase III program, right? So a patient gets a biopsy, we take those cells and make a product. We -- for those that are randomized to the intervention group, we inject one kidney and then 3 months later we inject the other kidney. That's the same as Group 1 in REGEN-007. And of the 24 people that were treated in 007 in Group 1, I think it was 16 -- 15 or 16 -- I think it was 16 subjects who had the key eligibility criteria for Phase III.

In short, if ProKidney can repeat the Group 1 results in its similar Phase 3 study, it seems likely the FDA would approve rilparencel in a multi-billion dollar market. Some analysts have suggested that, if approved, rilparencel could be a $900m per annum peak-selling product.

Nevertheless, in my last note I assigned the stock a Hold rating, writing:

That's not quite enough for me to join today's rally, however, because, as mentioned, I'd anticipate a dilutive fundraising happening, and also because biotech stocks' values do tend to decline when there are no near-term catalysts in play, and finally, because I still harbor some doubts that the Phase 3 study will be successful.

In fact, ProKidney did not complete a fundraising after sharing its data, and its share price has drifted downward, priced at $2.7 at the time of writing - down >50% since my note, and giving the company a market cap valuation of ~$800m.

Given the market opportunity in play, there appears to be an intriguing investment opportunity in play also, albeit a slow-burning one, In the remainder of this post, I'll update on recent developments and speculate about ProKidney's chance of recapturing former highs of >$5 per share, and even >$10 per share, shortly after listing, or whether the risks outweigh the potential reward.

ProKidney - Updates Since My Last Note Are Generally Promising

ProKidney announced its Q2 earnings August 12th. The company reported a cash position of $294.7m, versus $328.5m in at the end of Q1, R&D expenses of $25.9m, G&A expenses of $14m, and a net loss of $(37m).

During the recent fireside chat, ProKidney Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") James Coulston explained that:

As of end of June, we had $295 million of cash that takes us to mid-2027. What's important about that date is we have Phase III data and accelerated approval in the second quarter. So we do have cash to Phase III data.

In response to a question around what had changed financing wise (the company had formerly indicated its cash would not last to the end of the Phase 3), and whether the study had been adapted to reduce costs, the CFO commented:

Not specifically on the cost side, no. We've always been opportunistic about raising additional capital. We've got an ATM that we can utilize as needed, and then we're certainly again, opportunistic around raising additional capital and bringing additional capital into the company. But other than being very, very cautious with how we spend our cash, nothing specific on the cost side.

Management did opt to halt its second Phase 3 study, however, Proact 2, deeming it surplus to requirements after agreeing with the FDA that data from Proact 1 would be sufficient to file for accelerated approval, which it estimates has saved >$150m.

A Proact 1 study overview is provided below:

The company says that "more than half of the approximately 350 patients required for the accelerated approval analysis using eGFR slope have been enrolled as of August 2025", however there will be a lengthy wait for a topline data readout, which has been promised in Q2 2027.

There is a nearer term data catalyst to be aware of, however - full results from the REGEN-007 study are expected to be shared at the American Society of Nephrology ("ASN") Kidney Week in early November. During his recent fireside chat, the CEO hinted at a positive outcome:

And of the 24 people that were treated in 007 in Group 1, I think it was 16 -- 15 or 16 -- I think it was 16 subjects who had the key eligibility criteria for Phase III.

We haven't revealed what those results were in that group. But I can tell you, there's no difference between that group and the overall Group 1. If anything, it looks tiny bit better.

Happy to say that we submitted our late-breaking trial abstract last week. We submitted our manuscript last week. So now it's in the hands of the reviewers, and we'll see what happens.

The company also hopes to show more data discussing rilparencel's mechanism of action ("MoA"), an overview of which is provided in the slide below:

Interestingly, the CEO also commented on how he sees the market opportunity for rilparencel, explaining:

Now we and the FDA also believe that not everyone will either tolerate standard of care, may have some side effects or may be contraindications to ACE inhibitors or ARBs or SGLT2s or GLP-1 agonist. So we believe that our therapy is going to be on top of standard of care, which makes sense for those patients that end up with a GFR less than 35 despite best efforts of nephrologist and despite best efforts of patient compliance, et cetera, et cetera. So that's where we think we'll end up.

The CFO added that:

When you think about Stage IV CKD patients in IIb with high UACR, we're looking at a little over 1 million patients. And you factor in diabetics to that, we're closer to about 500,000 patients. Bruce also mentioned our ability to manufacture. We have our own manufacturing capabilities in North Carolina, capable of manufacturing for our Phase III program and also capable of manufacturing for our commercial launch

Analysis - A Slow Burner, But ProKidney Strikes Me As Destined For Success

ProKidney says it does not expect to move into earlier lines of treatment, and put itself in direct competition to GLP-1 drugs like Novo Nordisk' (NVO) Wegovy, which earned >$3bn last quarter, primarily in the obesity indication, although it is now approved to treat CKD, Dapagliflozin, marketed and sold by AstraZeneca (AZN), as Farxiga, or Empagliflozin, marketed and sold by Eli Lilly as Jardiance, both also multi-billion per annum selling assets.

Nevertheless, the case for using rilparencel to treat more severe stage patients does seem to have been persuasively made by the Group 1 REGEN-007 data - while the Group 2 cohort did not reach statistical significance, this group received a single injection, only receiving a second if a sustained decline in kidney function was observed.

Estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is a calculation from a blood test that estimates how much blood your kidneys filter per minute, and seemingly, in stage 4 CKD, rilparencel may be able to achieve results that no other therapy can - I'll reshare the same chart I used in my last note, showing that current standards of care do not have an adequate effect in this field:

As such, while there is a lengthy and nervous wait for investors to see the Phase 3 topline data, due in 2027, my assessment of the risk/reward is positive, i.e., I am of the opinion an investment in ProKidney may pay off when those results arrive, as they will support an accelerated approval (an approval based on the EGFR biomarker, with a further post-confirmatory study required to demonstrate actual clinical benefit).

The fact that ProKidney does not need to raise funding, that it no longer has to run two Phase 3 studies, that the latest REGEN-007 is expected to be presented soon, and is expected to be positive, the unmet need, alignment with the FDA, and market opportunity all give me encouragement.

Of course, there are risks to consider. There is no funding in place for a commercial launch, although ProKidney does have an ATM facility, permitting it to raise funds is necessary. There is high single-asset risk, as the company has no other programs. And there is no guarantee that positive data shown in Phase 2 will be replicated in a larger, Phase 3 study.

Investors should carefully consider these risks, in my view, before making any decisions, as there is no doubt that an investment in ProKidney could backfire and result in heavy losses. Nevertheless, on the balance of evidence discussed above, I am awarding ProKidney stock a Buy rating today.

data.title: APEX Global Solutions Targets $7 Million IPO Despite Flat Growth
data.description:
APEX Global’s Growth Is Flatlining

APEX Global Solutions Limited (APEX) has filed to raise $6.75 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

APEX provides corrosion prevention services to the marine industry in Singapore.

The company has almost stopped growing, has high geographic and customer concentration risks, and the valuation expectations of management are excessive.

My outlook on the APEX IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does APEX Global Do?

Singapore-based APEX Global Solutions Limited was founded to develop various corrosion prevention and related maintenance solutions for marine vessels at shipyards throughout Singapore.

Management is led by founder, Chairman and CEO Mr. Goh Kwang Yong, who has been with the firm since founding its subsidiary in 2009 and was previously founder of the Hypex Group of Companies in Singapore and has long expertise in the marine and oil & gas industries.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Corrosion prevention.

  • Surface preparation.

  • Maintenance solutions.

  • Hydro and grit blasting.

  • Repair services.

  • Manpower supply services.

APEX Global has booked fair market value investment of $1.5 million as of December 31, 2024, from investors, including the founder, Mr. Wan Hwee Chein and Triggen Holdings Limited.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have slightly increased, as the figures below indicate:

General & Administrative

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024

32.1%

Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023

34.5%

(Source: SEC).

The General & Administrative efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of General & Administrative expense, was only 0.1x in the most recent reporting period, an unimpressive result. (Source: SEC).

What Is APEX Global’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Market Research Future, the maritime sector in Singapore was an estimated $5 billion in total size in 2024 and is expected to exceed $8 billion by 2034.

This represents a projected CAGR of 5% from 2025 to 2034, a moderate growth rate at best.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are Singapore's strategic location, advanced port facilities and related infrastructure and pro-development policies by the government.

Also, the chart below illustrates the projected growth path of the overall maritime industry through 2034:

The marine corrosion and maintenance market in which APEX Global operates is a subset of the overall maritime market.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Aerostar Group.

  • Allbest Group.

  • Beng Kuang Group.

  • BoilerMaster Group.

  • Clavon Engineering.

  • Jeneric Group.

  • Mantech.

  • SHS (See Hup Seng).

  • Shipblast Marine Enterprise Pte Ltd.

  • SL Group.

  • Soh Tong Heng Group.

The company seeks clients from the industries of marine operators, offshore companies and industrial sector firms.

Most of its revenue comes from its corrosion prevention and maintenance service offerings, as the revenue contribution pie chart shows here:

APEX Global’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Barely growing top-line revenue.

  • Increased gross profit and gross margin.

  • Higher operating profit.

  • A swing to positive cash flow from operations.

Below are major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source: SEC).

As of December 31, 2024, APEX Global had $1.5 million in cash and equivalents and $4.3 million in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, of $1.0 million.

APEX Global’s IPO Plan

APEX intends to sell 1.5 million shares of Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $6.75 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No new shareholders have indicated any interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, from the filed prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

The sole class B shareholder (Founder Yong) will be entitled to twenty (20) votes per share and will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

The company, shareholders, senior executive and 5% or greater shareholders will be subject to the standard 180-day lock-up period after the effective date of the prospectus.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $3.71, which is within the typical range for a company at IPO.

The company’s enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $116 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 5.66%, meaning the stock will be considered a ‘low float’ stock subject to potentially much higher volatility in open market trading.

According to APEX Global’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

approximately 60%, or $2.64 million, to fund business expansion;

approximately 40%, or $1.76 million, to fund working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC).

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is available until the IPO is completed.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, as of December 31, 2024, the company had no material contingencies and believes that any legal claims would not have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or business.

The sole listed underwriter of the IPO is US Tiger Securities.

Here is a table of relevant capitalization and valuation figures for the firm:

(Source: SEC).

I’ve compared APEX Global to a public basket of related companies as compiled by noted valuation expert Dr. Aswath Damodaran, with the results shown here:

(Source: NYU Stern School/Dr. Aswath Damodaran).

While the Shipbuilding & Marine category is wide, Apex appears to be seeking extremely high valuation multiples at IPO despite a very low topline growth rate.

APEX Global Is Hardly Growing But Profitable

APEX seeks U.S. public capital investment for its general growth plans.

The firm’s financials have generated slightly growing topline revenue, higher gross profit and gross margin, increased operating profit and a swing to positive cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, was $1.0 million.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen slightly as revenue has barely grown and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple was only 0.1x in the most recent full calendar year.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends in the near future, although it has recently paid dividends to its existing investors.

APEX’s recent capital spending history shows it has avoided spending on capital expenditures in the most recent calendar year.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its markets overseas, growing its service offerings and improving its operational efficiency.

The market opportunity for providing corrosion prevention and related maintenance services in Singapore is moderate in size and expected to grow in the coming years.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, which allows management to disclose substantially less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly immediately after their IPO.

The company has geographic concentration risks since all of its revenue comes from Singapore.

It also had only three (3) customers in 2024 that accounted for 61% of its total revenue, so it has high customer concentration risks.

The firm also had material related party transactions, greater than 10% of revenues, in the most recent calendar year, as shown in more detail here.

As to valuation, APEX Global appears to be seeking high valuation multiples despite almost no revenue growth.

Given the firm’s concentration and other risks, excessive valuation assumptions, and flattening of revenue growth, my outlook on the APEX IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Fanuc: Consider Market Share Loss, Favorable Industry Data (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I'm downgrading my rating of Fanuc Corporation (OTCPK:FANUY) (OTCPK:FANUF) (6954.T) from a "Buy" to a "Hold" now. From my perspective, a stock is a compelling "Buy" only if its major businesses are doing equally well. This isn't the case for this name.

My earlier July 7, 2025 write-up previewed FANUY's 1QFY25 (YE Mar) results and analyzed its shareholder return.

Robots Business Is Ceding Ground To Chinese Rival

FANUY saw its contribution to the PRC's industrial robot sales fall from 11% in 2024 to 9% for 2Q2025, based on MIR data (paid research not publicly available).

The firm used to be the No.1 player, but it has since relinquished the title to a local player. China's Estun (002747.SZ) grew its domestic sales volume by 44% year-on-year in April-June '25, which translated into an industry-leading market share of 10.5%. This could signify a major inflection point. Before this, the leading robot maker in the Chinese region has always been a foreign company.

Estun took part in an investor meeting (S&P Capital IQ transcript) on Sept. 12, 2025. It credited its success of late to the "rapid growth in end-markets such as automobiles, electronics, and lithium batteries."

Morgan Stanley (MS) published a report (inaccessible in public) titled "Factory Automation Investment Is A Structural Necessity" on Sept 10. The bank's research estimates that FANUY is responsible for roughly between a tenth and one-fifth of industrial robots sold worldwide now. It is likely to be a key victim of Estun's lofty ambitions.

Estun has set a goal to account for 10% of the global robot sector by the end of the current decade. I think there's a good chance that this could be achieved.

A Sept. 9 SA News article noted that "BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY) and other Chinese companies are expanding in the UK and other European markets by providing reasonably priced EVs." This means Estun can ride on its client's overseas growth to broaden its international presence. It also mentioned at the September investment event that it's in the process of getting qualified by certain Tier-1 "European Union" or "EU" automakers.

FANUY shared at the 1QFY2025 analyst briefing on July 25 that "in Europe, ROBOTs (operations) have the highest sales" among the various units within the group. It worries me that Fanuc might continue to lose business to Estun in other parts of the world, taking reference from the challenges in China.

But Factory Automation Unit Is Anticipated To Grow Faster

The firm's two biggest divisions are "ROBOTs" and "Factory Automation/FA," representing 41% and 25% of its latest quarterly top-line, respectively.

FANUY's robot segment underperformed with a 3.8% YoY revenue contraction in 1QFY25. This wasn't unexpected, considering competitive pressures, which I outlined in the preceding section. On the flip side, the FA business registered a 3.5% sales increase last quarter. My take is that its near-term prospects are favorable.

Japan's aggregate "machine tool" orders (both domestic and exports) amounted to ¥0.12T in August '25. This implies a growth acceleration from 2Q25's (calendar year) 3% to 8% last month.

Separately, Japanese firm Citizen Watch Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:CHCLY) (OTCPK:CHCLF) recently met with Citigroup's (C) analysts. Management's disclosures were detailed in a research note (only available for C's clients) with the title "Machine Tool Business Update," dated Sept. 15. CHCLY highlighted that the "recovery" in the sales of its "CNC automatic lathes" is "materializing earlier than expected."

In my March 6, 2025 piece, I explained that "FANUF's FA unit sells CNC (Computer Numerical Control) equipment, which is used by manufacturers of machinery tools."

FANUY's 1QFY25 call commentary stressed that its FA segment is benefiting from an "increase in orders by Chinese machine tool builders and increase in demands in emerging markets." The positive expectations of foreign markets are consistent with a 12% jump in Japan's total "machine tool" exports for August.

I'm anticipating that Fanuc's factory automation business should expand at a more rapid rate in 2QFY25 (Jul-Sep) vis-a-vis Q1FY2025. This takes into consideration sector statistics and corporate disclosures cited above.

MIR's statistics also indicate that FANUY is the outright leader in China's CNC systems boasting a 30% market share. As the quality of CNCs has a direct effect on production efficiency and output, the threat from factory automation rivals offering cheaper substitutes is not a major concern.

My Closing Thoughts

I have a mixed opinion of Fanuc as a potential investment candidate. There's a divergence in the outlooks for its key segments. FANUY's Robot division has fallen behind its rivals in the PRC. But it's dominant in the factory automation space, and the latest monthly industry numbers have encouraging read-throughs.

I think FANUY's shares are trading at fair value with a 19.5 times forward EV/EBIT. It appears reasonable based on peer/historical comparisons, which are pretty close. The stock's own all-time trading average is 20.3x. Japanese industrial company Keyence's (OTCPK:KYCCF) (6861.T) EBIT multiple is 20.9x.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Affinity Bancshares: No Dividend, But Buying Back Its Own Shares
data.description:
Introduction

I last discussed Affinity Bancshares (NASDAQ:AFBI) in May of this year, after the company’s sale was canceled. Affinity Bancshares is the holding company of Affinity Bank, focusing on Georgia, where it has its main branch in Newton County. As I recently decided to turn my focus to US-based local and regional banks (after taking profits on my investments in the financial sector in Europe), Affinity Bancshares was on my list to have another look, and now the company has disclosed an additional six months of earnings.

Robust increase in the net interest income

During the second quarter of the year, the bank reported a 5% increase in its interest income compared to the second quarter of last year. And while the interest expenses increased by approximately $0.6M, the interest expenses increased as well, but fortunately by just under $0.4M. This means the net interest income on a pre-provision basis increased by just under 3% or $0.2M, to $7.78M in the second quarter of this year.

As the income statement above shows, the total amount of provisions to cover soured loans has dropped to almost nothing, and after recording $50,000 in the first quarter of the year, the bank only needed about $17,000 in the second quarter of the year. It goes without saying that’s a positive evolution for the bank’s general profitability.

The bank was also able to reduce its non-interest expenses as it no longer incurred any expenses related to the failed buyout by the Atlanta Postal Credit Union (APCU), and this resulted in a pre-tax income of $2.8M and a net profit of $2.15M for an EPS of $0.34. That’s more than twice the $0.16 in EPS generated in the second quarter of last year thanks to a substantial increase in the reported net income, in combination with a 1.5% decrease in the share count.

This brought the H1 net income to just under $4M, resulting in an EPS of $0.63. That’s based on the weighted average share count, while the net share count as of August 8 th was about 1.1% lower.

Affinity Bancshares currently does not pay a dividend, so it should be able to increase its book value pretty fast.

A closer look at the loan book and the loans past due

When I see a bank is able to keep its loan loss provisions limited, I’m always curious to see the breakdown of the loan book, and what percentage of the loans is classified as ‘past due’.

Looking at the asset side of the balance sheet, the bank has approximately $934M in assets, of which just under $90M is held in cash with an additional $40.7M in securities available for sale as well as just over $24.4M in securities held to maturity. This is also one of the very few cases where I have seen the fair value of securities HTM is higher than the book value, and in Affinity’s case, the fair value of this asset class is approximately $0.4M higher than the book value. All things considered, Affinity’s portfolio is fairly liquid, but I am of course most interested in the $731M loan portfolio.

As you can see below, the bank’s $731M loan book primarily consists of commercial real estate, which makes up about 45% of the pre-provision loan book size. This is followed by the $148M in commercial and business loans.

Looking at the total amount of loans past due and the non-accruing loans, it’s perhaps surprising to see the largest category where loans are (at least) past due is the residential mortgage category. Although this category is less than 1/6 th of the CRE segment, the total amount of non-accruing loans is higher than in both CRE segments combined. It will be interesting to see how the bank deals with this going forward, but as these loans are backed by real estate, I am hopeful the bank won’t need to record a substantial write-down as it should be able to recoup the majority of its exposure by monetizing the assets.

It is quite unfortunate Affinity Bancshares has not disclosed average Loan-to-Value ratios across its real estate portfolio, but it did mention that the almost $40M in non-owner occupied office loans had an average LTV ratio of less than 50%. So even if the office segment (which includes medical tenants) would come under more pressure, Affinity should be relatively shielded from the fallout.

Investment thesis

As Affinity Bancshares is a relatively small bank, it doesn’t provide as much detail on its financial results as larger banks do, and I think it would be interesting to get some more color on the average LTV ratios in the portfolio, and how the bank plans to deal with the loans that are currently no longer accruing.

The bank ended the first semester with a tangible book value of approximately $16.80 per share, which means its current share price represents a premium of 18 which is very reasonable. And although the current share price represents approximately 13-14 times the annualized earnings based on the H1 result, Affinity Bancshares could be seen as a ‘buy’ right now although perhaps a bit on the more speculative side, as I would like to see the reported earnings continue to increase/improve.

I currently have no position in Affinity Bancshares, but as I have been focusing on US regional and local banks lately, I will keep closer tabs on Affinity going forward.
